text: 60.8g of fat. omega3 is where things start to fall off. for nuts, fat quality has to justify the calorie density.
16.7g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for a nut, you need enough upside to make the density worth it.
15g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is support here, not the whole argument.
vitamin e at 100% daily value stands out most. that is major vitamin support.
magnesium at 41% daily value, zinc at 18% daily value, iron at 26% daily value stand out most. useful, but still not a major mineral edge.

pros first: portable, practical snack food because nuts score real points in life because they are easy to use. fat and texture help satiety because they can hold up better than many snack foods. still a recognisable whole-food option because that keeps them cleaner than most processed snacks.

the drawbacks next: calorie density is very high because that makes portion control a real issue. protein is useful but not elite because nuts help, but they are not protein stars. cost or allergy issues can limit use because practical downsides still count.
C tier. Best only in small amounts when the calories stay under control.
